raw_consumer_statement:
I am a Texas resident disputing XXXX re-posted charges from XXXX XXXX totaling {$420.00} ( {$280.00} and {$140.00} ) on my Barclays credit card ( Confirmation # XXXX ). 
The Dispute : 'Original Form of Payment ' vs. Restrictive Credits XXXX XXXX is attempting to deny a cash refund by claiming the ticket was purchased with a 'Reservation Credit. ' I formally reject this. Under USDOT 14 CFR Part 260, when a carrier initiates a significant change ( 3+ hours ), they must refund the passenger to the 'original form of payment. ' Because the underlying value of this credit originated from a cash purchase on my Barclays credit card ending in XXXX, the law mandates the airline make me whole by returning those funds to that card. 
Carrier Failure & Incurred Damages XXXX changed my XXXX XXXX  departure to XXXX XXXX  ( a XXXX, XXXX change ). No other flights were available on that calendar day. Because XXXX failed to provide the contracted service, I was forced to purchase a replacement flight on XXXX XXXX at my own expense to reach my destination. I have provided Barclays with the receipt for this replacement flight as proof of service failure. 
Illegal Procedural Stalling XXXX has stated they require 60 days to 'research ' this matter. However, 14 CFR 260.10 requires credit card refunds be processed within 7 business days. By re-posting these charges to my statement while simultaneously claiming a 60-day window, XXXX is practicing 'unjust enrichment'holding my funds hostage for a service they never rendered. 
Requested Resolution : I request that the CFPB compel Barclays to permanently reverse these XXXX charges ( {$280.00} and {$140.00} ) and any associated interest. Barclays should not allow a merchant 's internal 'credit ' policy to override federal consumer protection laws regarding mandatory cash refunds for significant carrier-initiated changes.
